title: Dog Labored Breathing and Abdominal Heaving: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog has labored breathing and abdominal heaving, it is a medical emergency. Learn why this happens and what immediate steps you must take.
content_html:
What is Labored Breathing with Abdominal Heaving?

Labored breathing, medically known as dyspnea, occurs when a dog is struggling to move air into or out of their lungs. When abdominal heaving is present, it indicates that the dog is recruiting the muscles in their stomach to help force air in and out because the diaphragm and chest muscles are no longer sufficient. This is a clear sign of respiratory distress and suggests that your dog is not receiving adequate oxygen.

Urgency Level: High (Immediate Emergency)

Abdominal heaving is considered a critical medical emergency. If your dog is using their belly to breathe, their oxygen saturation levels are likely dropping to dangerous levels. This condition can be caused by heart failure, pneumonia, trauma, or fluid in the chest (pleural effusion). You should contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately. Do not wait to see if the symptoms improve, as respiratory failure can occur rapidly.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

While your priority should be getting your dog to the vet, capturing a short 5 to 10-second video of the breathing pattern can be vital for the veterinary team. A video allows the triage nurse or veterinarian to see the rhythm and effort of the breaths, helping them prepare the necessary oxygen therapy or diagnostic equipment before you even walk through the door. If you are alone, do not waste time filming; prioritize transport above all else.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of abdominal breathing in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include congestive heart failure, asthma, pneumonia, physical trauma to the chest, or the ingestion of toxins.

faqs.question: Should I try to perform CPR if my dog stops breathing?
faqs.answer: If your dog becomes unconscious and stops breathing, you can attempt chest compressions, but it is vital to be en route to an emergency clinic while doing so.

faqs.question: Can stress cause abdominal heaving?
faqs.answer: While stress can cause panting, true abdominal heaving is usually a sign of an underlying physiological issue. It should never be dismissed as just stress or anxiety.
